extra.message: User input from request.POST['name'] and request.POST['pass'] are concatenated directly into a raw SQL string passed to login.objects.raw(). An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL, e.g. setting name to ' OR '1'='1.

User input from request.POST['name'] and request.POST['pass'] are concatenated directly into a raw SQL string passed to sql_lab_table.objects.raw(). This allows full SQL injection including UNION-based and boolean-based attacks.

User-supplied 'domain' input (after a weak protocol-stripping regex) is interpolated into a shell command string ('nslookup {}' or 'dig {}') and executed with shell=True via subprocess.Popen. The regex only strips protocol/www prefixes but does not prevent injection of shell metacharacters. An attacker can inject commands such as '; cat /etc/passwd'.
User-supplied 'val' from request.POST is passed directly to Python's built-in eval(). This allows arbitrary Python code execution on the server, leading to full remote code execution.

cwe: CWE-95: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code (Eval Injection)

extra.message: User-supplied 'expression' from request.POST is passed directly to Python's built-in eval() without any authentication check (@csrf_exempt is applied and the authentication_decorator is commented out). This allows unauthenticated arbitrary code execution.

cwe: CWE-95: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code (Eval Injection)

extra.message: User-supplied 'ip' from request.POST is concatenated directly into a shell command string ('nmap ' + ip) and executed with shell=True via subprocess.Popen. An attacker can inject shell metacharacters to execute arbitrary OS commands.

The 'token' cookie value is base64-decoded and passed directly to pickle.loads() without any integrity check or signature verification. An attacker who can forge or modify the cookie can supply a malicious pickle payload to achieve remote code execution.

User-supplied 'serialized_data' form field is base64-decoded and passed directly to pickle.loads() without any integrity check. An attacker can craft a malicious pickle payload to achieve remote code execution.

The SAX parser is explicitly configured with feature_external_ges=True, enabling external general entity processing. User-supplied XML from request.body is then parsed with this parser. This allows XXE attacks including local file disclosure (e.g., /etc/passwd) and SSRF.

cwe: CWE-611: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference

extra.message: User-supplied 'blog' parameter from request.POST is joined with the application's base directory via os.path.join() and used to open a file for reading. No path validation or canonicalization is performed, allowing path traversal attacks (e.g., '../../../../etc/passwd') to read arbitrary files on the server.

The 'file' parameter passed to ssrf_lab() is joined with the playground directory path via os.path.join() and opened for reading without any validation. When called from the ssrf_code_checker API with user-controlled test bench inputs (including 'secret.txt'), this allows traversal to read arbitrary files relative to the playground directory.

User-supplied 'url' from request.POST is passed directly to requests.get() without any URL validation or allowlist. This allows Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF), enabling an attacker to make the server issue requests to internal network services, cloud metadata endpoints (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/), or other arbitrary URLs.

User-supplied 'blog' content from request.POST is processed by filter_blog() which performs no filtering (it returns the input unchanged), then written directly into a Django template file on disk. The template is later rendered via render(). This allows Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI): an attacker can inject Django template syntax such as '{{ request.META }}' or use template tags to read server-side data.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code

extra.message: A user-uploaded YAML file is loaded with yaml.load(file, yaml.Loader). Using yaml.Loader (the full loader) allows deserialization of arbitrary Python objects via YAML tags (e.g., !!python/object/apply:os.system), leading to remote code execution. yaml.safe_load() should be used instead.

User-supplied 'function' from request.POST is passed directly to PIL.ImageMath.eval(). In Pillow versions prior to 10.0.0, ImageMath.eval() internally calls Python's eval() with the expression, allowing arbitrary code execution. Even in newer versions this is a dangerous pattern.

cwe: CWE-95: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code (Eval Injection)

extra.message: In xss_lab2, user-supplied 'username' from request.POST has only '' tags removed (bypassable with case variations like '

In xss_lab3, user-supplied 'username' from request.POST has only alphanumeric characters removed via regex, deliberately allowing special characters ([]()!+) for JSFuck-style payloads. The result is passed to the template as 'code', allowing XSS via character-encoded JavaScript payloads.

The auth_lab_signup view sets a 'userid' cookie containing the database primary key of the newly created user (an integer). This cookie is used for authentication in auth_lab_login by directly looking up the user by this ID. Any user can forge this cookie to authenticate as any other user by guessing or enumerating sequential integer user IDs (IDOR).

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: In ba_lab, admin access is granted if the client-controlled cookie 'admin' equals '1'. An attacker can simply set this cookie to '1' in their browser to gain admin access and see the secret key without any valid credentials.
In a1_broken_access_lab_1, admin access is granted if the client-controlled cookie 'admin' equals '1'. An attacker can set this cookie to bypass authentication and access protected data.

In a1_broken_access_lab_2, admin access is granted by checking if HTTP_USER_AGENT equals 'pygoat_admin'. The User-Agent header is fully attacker-controlled and can be trivially spoofed. This is a broken access control using an untrusted HTTP header for authorization.

The a1_broken_access_lab3_secret view only checks that the user is authenticated (is_authenticated) but performs no check that the user has admin privileges. Any authenticated user can access the secret page directly by navigating to /broken_access_controle/secret.

The csrf_transfer_monei_api view performs a money transfer (modifies account balances) in response to a GET request and has no CSRF protection. The transfer is triggered via a URL like /mitre/9/lab/api/<recipient>/<amount> with only cookie-based JWT auth. A cross-site link or image tag can cause authenticated users to transfer funds without their consent.

User-supplied password is hashed using MD5 (from hashlib) before being compared against the stored hash in the CF_user table. MD5 is a cryptographically broken algorithm for password storage: it is fast, making it trivially brute-forceable, and has known collision vulnerabilities.

cwe: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

extra.message: Password is hashed with MD5 before being stored/compared in the CSRF_user_tbl. MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsuitable for password hashing.

cwe: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

extra.message: In crypto_failure_lab3, a plaintext cookie of the format 'username|expiry_datetime' is set without any HMAC or signature. An attacker can modify the cookie value to change the username to 'admin' and extend the expiry, gaining admin access. The cookie is also not set with the HttpOnly or Secure flags.

cwe: CWE-565: Reliance on Cookies Without Validation and Integrity Checking

extra.message: The JWT token for the CSRF lab is signed with a hardcoded secret key 'csrf_vulneribility'. An attacker who knows this key (which is visible in source) can forge arbitrary JWT tokens to impersonate any user.

The Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as a static string 'lr66%-a!$km5ed@n5ug!tya5bv!0(yqwa1tn!q%0%3m2nh%oml'. This key is committed to source control and is used for cryptographic signing of sessions, cookies, and CSRF tokens. An attacker with access to the source can forge sessions and CSRF tokens.

SECRET_COOKIE_KEY is hardcoded as 'PYGOAT' in settings.py. This key is used to sign JWT auth cookies in sec_misconfig_lab3. An attacker can forge JWT tokens with this known secret to gain admin access.

DEBUG = True is set in the Django settings file. In debug mode, Django exposes detailed exception tracebacks including local variable values, settings, and installed apps to any user who triggers an error. This leaks sensitive information including stack traces and configuration details.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code

extra.message: DEBUG = True is set in the sensitive_data_lab Django settings. In debug mode, Django exposes detailed exception tracebacks to end users.

cwe: CWE-215: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Debugging Code

extra.message: ALLOWED_HOSTS = ['*'] permits the application to accept requests with any Host header, bypassing Django's host header validation protection and potentially enabling host header injection attacks.
Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as 'django-insecure-key-for-demonstration-only' in the sensitive_data_lab settings. This key is used for session signing and CSRF tokens.

The all_users_data_view function returns all users' credit card numbers, SSNs, and API keys as JSON with no authentication or authorization check. Any unauthenticated client can call this endpoint to harvest the full PII dataset of all registered users.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The api_data_view endpoint returns the authenticated user's unmasked credit card number, full SSN, and API key as plaintext JSON. Sensitive financial data should never be transmitted in full; only masked representations should be returned.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: Hardcoded plaintext credentials for 'admin' (password: 'admin123') and 'user' (password: 'password123') are stored in an in-memory dictionary. Passwords are compared in plaintext at login without hashing, and stored in plaintext in the users dict.

Session tokens are generated as base64-encoded strings of 'username:datetime.now()'. This is not cryptographically secure: the token is trivially forgeable (just base64-encode any username), and base64 is not encryption. The dashboard validates sessions by simply base64-decoding the cookie and trusting the embedded username.
Flask secret_key is hardcoded as 'your-secret-key-here'. Flask uses this key to sign session cookies; a known secret key allows an attacker to forge session cookies.

Flask application is started with debug=True. In debug mode, the Werkzeug debugger is enabled, which provides an interactive Python console accessible from the browser if an exception occurs, allowing arbitrary code execution. The debugger PIN can be brute-forced or bypassed.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code

extra.message: The OTP is generated as a 3-digit integer (100-999, only 900 possible values) via randint() and exposed directly in the HTTP response body for non-admin users. There is no rate limiting, no attempt lockout, and the email used for lookup is taken from an attacker-controlled cookie. An attacker can enumerate all 900 possible OTPs trivially.

cwe: CWE-307: Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts

extra.message: In auth_lab_signup, the user's plaintext password from request.POST is stored directly in the authLogin model's password field without hashing. This means passwords are stored in cleartext in the database.

cwe: CWE-256: Plaintext Storage of a Password

extra.message: In the register route, the user's plaintext password is stored directly in the in-memory users dictionary without any hashing. Passwords are never hashed in this application.

cwe: CWE-256: Plaintext Storage of a Password

extra.message: The X-Forwarded-For header value (fully attacker-controlled) is interpolated into log messages at INFO level without sanitization. An attacker can inject newlines and arbitrary content into log files (log injection), potentially poisoning log analysis tools or forging log entries.

cwe: CWE-117: Improper Output Neutralization for Logs

extra.message: The log_function_checker API (decorated with @csrf_exempt but NOT @authentication_decorator, as the decorator is commented out on line 60) accepts arbitrary Python code via request.POST and writes it directly to playground/A9/main.py and playground/A9/api.py. An unauthenticated attacker can overwrite these Python module files with malicious code that is subsequently imported and executed by the application.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code

extra.message: The A6_disscussion_api_2 endpoint (CSRF-exempt, requires POST) accepts arbitrary code via request.POST and writes it directly to introduction/playground/A6/utility.py. Any authenticated user can overwrite this module file with malicious code that will be executed the next time the module is imported or a function from it is called.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code

extra.message: USER_A7_LAB3 is a hardcoded dictionary containing SHA-256 password hashes for User1-User4. These hashes are fixed and stored in plaintext in source code, allowing offline cracking. The comment acknowledges these are hardcoded credentials 'for demonstration purpose only'.
In a1_broken_access_lab_3, credentials are hardcoded in the view: 'John'/'reaper' and 'admin'/'admin_pass'. These plaintext credentials are committed to source control.
In a1_broken_access_lab_1, a hardcoded credential check 'jack'/'jacktheripper' is present in the view code.
